summary: Spolsky draws on Ray Oldenburg's concept of 'third places' and Robert Putnam's 'Bowling Alone' to argue that modern society, especially among software developers, suffers from a critical lack of social gathering spaces. He observes that programmers often flock to online communities out of loneliness, making community software a digital attempt at creating third places. The post argues that design decisions in community software are as consequential as architectural decisions in physical spaces, shaping whether communities thrive or wither. This is a teaser for his longer article 'Building Communities with Software.'
key_points: Humans need a 'third place' beyond work and home for social interaction, but capitalist society has been steadily eroding these spaces
Software developers are particularly affected by the loss of third places, often relocating to new cities and working long hours out of loneliness
Online communities (forums, chat rooms, open source projects) serve as surrogate third places for isolated programmers
Design decisions in community software directly shape behavior and determine whether a community feels welcoming or deserted, just as physical architecture shapes social interaction
money_quotes: For too many people, life consists of going to work, then going home and watching TV. Work-TV-Sleep-Work-TV-Sleep.
People graduate from college, move across country to a new place where they don't know anyone, and end up working 12 hour days basically out of loneliness.
So it's no surprise that so many programmers, desperate for a little human contact, flock to online communities.
In creating community software, we are, to some extent, trying to create a third place.
When you make something easy, people do it more often. When you make something hard, people do it less often.
In this way you can gently encourage people to behave in certain ways which determine the character and quality of the community.
An ersatz third place is less painful than none at all.
In software, as in architecture, design decisions are just as important to the type of community that develops or fails to develop.
key_insight: Community software design decisions function like architectural choices in physical spaces — they subtly shape human behavior and determine whether an online community becomes a thriving third place or a deserted wasteland.
